agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an e-commerce/logistics system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have each provided explicit requirements that, taken together, create a logical impossibility around when—and whether—an order can be cancelled.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase and receive goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states from placement to delivery."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so that it will not be fulfilled or shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document representing a binding charge for an order. Once created, it is irreversible per finance policy."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer, representing a point-of-no-return in the fulfillment lifecycle."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order, which per operations policy also triggers immediate invoicing to lock in pricing."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may be cancelled at any time before it has shipped.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a product/business policy defining the cancellation window. A VP could change the window (e.g., allow cancellation up to 1 hour before ship).","testable_assertion":"GIVEN an order in any pre-shipment state, WHEN a cancellation is requested, THEN the order transitions to Cancelled."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An invoiced order cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats the invoice as a binding financial commitment. Reversing it would corrupt the financial ledger. If cancellation is needed post-invoice, a separate credit note or refund process would be required—not a simple cancellation.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN an order that has been invoiced, WHEN a cancellation is requested, THEN the cancellation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to invoice. It could be changed to invoice at shipment or at a different trigger point.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a new order is placed, WHEN placement completes, THEN an invoice is immediately generated for that order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed but has not yet shipped","when":"The customer or an operator requests cancellation","then":"The system must determine whether cancellation is permitted based on the resolved cancellation policy (pending stakeholder alignment on BR-1 vs BR-2 conflict)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has already shipped","when":"A cancellation is requested","then":"The cancellation is rejected with a clear message that shipped orders cannot be cancelled"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order is being placed","when":"The placement process completes","then":"An invoice is generated and associated with the order (per current operations policy)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An invoice exists for an order","when":"Any process attempts to void or delete the invoice directly","then":"The operation is rejected; invoices are immutable financial records"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed but not yet shipped — customer requests cancellation immediately after placement","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTION: BR-1 says allow (not yet shipped), BR-2 says deny (already invoiced per BR-3). Behavior is undefined until stakeholders resolve the conflict."},{"scenario":"Order placed, invoice generated, but shipment fails or is delayed indefinitely","expected_behavior":"Under current rules the order is permanently uncancellable despite never shipping, which is likely not the intended business outcome."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether cancellation applies to unshipped items. Even if allowed by PM rule, those items are already invoiced."},{"scenario":"System failure causes invoice generation to be delayed after placement","expected_behavior":"A brief window where the order is placed but not invoiced. BR-1 would allow cancellation, BR-2 would not block it. This race condition reveals the architectural tension."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If cancellation is blocked on all placed orders (strict reading of BR-2 + BR-3), customers have zero cancellation ability, likely increasing chargebacks, refund disputes, and customer support costs. If cancellation is allowed (strict reading of BR-1), finance must handle invoice reversals or credit notes, adding accounting complexity.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. If we decouple invoicing from placement to resolve the contradiction, operations loses price-lock guarantees and must find another mechanism (e.g., price reservations).","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. A system that silently prevents all cancellations due to immediate invoicing would cause significant user frustration and erode trust.","risk":"HIGH — Implementing any interpretation without stakeholder alignment will either violate financial controls (allowing cancellation of invoiced orders) or violate customer expectations (preventing all cancellations). Either path carries regulatory, financial, or reputational risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — their ability to cancel orders is the core feature. Current rules make cancellation impossible in practice."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Must define whether a credit-note/refund workflow is acceptable as a substitute for direct invoice cancellation."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"May need to change when invoicing occurs, or accept a new 'price reservation' mechanism that locks pricing without generating an invoice."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of complaints if cancellation is blocked, or must process manual workarounds."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Must reconcile feature promise (cancel before ship) with financial and operational constraints."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other when composed. BR-1 (cancel before ship) + BR-3 (invoice at placement) + BR-2 (invoiced = uncancellable) yields: orders are uncancellable the moment they are placed, which nullifies the entire cancellation feature. The spec cannot be implemented as stated. Confidence is low because the correct behavior is unknowable without stakeholder resolution.","high_confidence_areas":["The three rules as stated are individually clear and unambiguous","The logical contradiction between the three rules is definitive, not a matter of interpretation","Invoice immutability is a sound financial invariant"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether finance would accept a credit-note workflow as distinct from 'cancelling an invoice'","Whether operations could decouple price-locking from invoicing","The intended order lifecycle state machine"],"assumptions":["All three stakeholder statements are current and authoritative","There is no existing credit-note or refund mechanism that finance considers distinct from invoice cancellation","Invoice generation is synchronous with order placement, not batched"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before ship) is logically incompatible with BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled) combined with BR-3 (orders are invoiced at placement). Together, BR-2 + BR-3 mean no order can ever be cancelled, which directly negates BR-1. The cancellation feature as specified is impossible to implement.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers"],"options":["A: Decouple invoicing from placement — invoice at shipment instead, preserving the cancellation window. Operations must find another price-lock mechanism (e.g., price reservation).","B: Introduce a credit-note/refund workflow — keep invoicing at placement but allow a financial reversal via credit note rather than invoice deletion. Finance must accept this as not violating invoice immutability.","C: Narrow the cancellation window — invoice is generated asynchronously (e.g., 30-minute delay), allowing a brief cancellation window. Partial fix, still conflicts with operations' stated policy.","D: Redefine 'cancellation' — a cancelled order triggers a refund process against the existing invoice rather than voiding it. Semantically different from 'invoice cancellation' and may satisfy all parties."],"recommendation":"Option B or D: Introduce a credit-note/refund mechanism that preserves invoice immutability while enabling order cancellation before shipment. This respects finance's ledger integrity, operations' price-lock timing, and the PM's cancellation requirement. Recommend convening all three stakeholders to validate."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated cannot all be satisfied simultaneously. Implementing any two violates the third.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 ∧ BR-2 ∧ BR-3 — logically inconsistent rule set"}]}

Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have each provided explicit requirements that, taken together, create a logical impossibility around when—and whether—an order can be cancelled.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase and receive goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states fro

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these requirements carefully.

Three statements:
1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

The contradiction is clear:
- If orders are invoiced at placement (ops), and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (finance), then orders can NEVER be cancelled (since they're invoiced immediately).
- But the PM says orders CAN be cancelled before shipping.

This is a direct contradiction between explicit stakeholder requirements. 

All three statements cannot coexist — at least one needs to change to resolve this conflict.

Together, BR-2 + BR-3 mean no order can ever be cancelled, which directly negates BR-1. The cancellation feature as specified is impossible to implement.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers"],"options":["A: Decouple invoicing from placement — invoice at shipment instead, preserving the cancellation window. Finance must accept this as not violating invoice immutability.","C: Narrow the cancellation window — invoice is generated asynchronously (e.g., 30-minute delay), allowing a brief cancellation window.
